did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper evaluated an mHealth application (SkinVision) that uses a rule-based algorithm incorporating fractal and classical image analysis to extract texture, color, and geometric features from skin lesion images for risk assessment of (pre)malignant conditions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled 'Original Research' in the title section. It presents a primary clinical study evaluating the sensitivity and specificity of the SkinVision mHealth application algorithm on a dataset of 341 lesions (239 with histopathology) collected at Catharina Hospital Eindhoven. The study includes specific sections for Background, Materials and Methods, Results, and Discussion, reporting original experimental data and statistical analysis (e.g., 80% sensitivity, 78% specificity) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: mHealth App for Risk Assessment of Pigmented and Nonpigmented Skin Lesions—A Study on Sensitivity and Specificity in Detecting Malignancy
brief_summary: This study evaluated the SkinVision mHealth application, which uses a rule-based algorithm incorporating fractal and classical image analysis, for the risk assessment of pigmented and nonpigmented skin lesions. The algorithm was tested on a dataset of 108 lesions collected at Catharina Hospital Eindhoven, with 239 lesions having histopathological confirmation. The main evaluation approach involved calculating sensitivity and specificity against clinical and histopathological diagnoses of (pre)malignant conditions. The recalibrated algorithm achieved a sensitivity of 80% and a specificity of 78% in detecting malignancy. The study concludes that while less accurate than a dermatologist's clinical eye, the app offers valuable support for nondermatologists in identifying signs of malignancy.

all_task_types.target: Risk category (High risk vs. Low/Medium risk) for (pre)malignant conditions
all_task_types.details: The algorithm classifies lesions into discrete risk categories ('high risk' if malignant or premalignant, 'medium or low risk' otherwise) based on image features and questionnaire inputs. Performance is evaluated using sensitivity and specificity for detecting (pre)malignant conditions.

datasets_used.name: Eindhoven Database (Catharina Hospital Eindhoven)
datasets_used.description: A prospective dataset of pigmented and nonpigmented melanocytic and nonmelanocytic lesions collected at the Department of Dermatology, Catharina Hospital Eindhoven, The Netherlands, between December 2014 and April 2016. Lesions were imaged using the SkinVision app and evaluated against clinical diagnosis and histopathological results.

use.training: 233 lesions were used as the training data set for the rule-based algorithm calibration.
use.testing: 108 lesions were used as the test data set for the algorithm’s evaluation in terms of specificity and sensitivity in detecting (pre)malignant lesions.

datasets_used.name: Ludwig Maximilian University (LMU) Database
datasets_used.description: A database of melanocytic lesions with histopathology used during the algorithm's first investigation. It contained 144 melanocytic lesions: 118 benign nevi and 26 melanomas. This dataset was used to evaluate the recalibrated algorithm's performance on melanocytic lesions specifically.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the recalibrated algorithm's sensitivity and specificity for melanoma detection against histopathological results.
use.other: Used for external comparison of algorithm performance (sensitivity increased from 73% to 88%, specificity dropped from 83% to ~79%).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty for sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value.
Online statistical software used to calculate sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value with 95% confidence intervals.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value with 95% confidence intervals calculated using VassarStats.

Eindhoven Database (Test Data Set).description: Evaluation of the recalibrated algorithm incorporating patient questionnaire answers versus clinical diagnosis on 108 lesions (35 malignant/premalignant, 73 benign).
Eindhoven Database (Test Data Set).notes: Out of 35 (pre)malignant lesions, 28 were rated high risk, 6 medium risk, and 1 low risk. All melanomas were rated high risk. Out of 73 benign lesions, 14 were rated high risk and 11 medium risk. One basal cell carcinoma was assessed as low risk.

Ludwig Maximilian University (LMU) Datab.description: Evaluation of the recalibrated algorithm versus histopathological results on 144 melanocytic lesions (26 melanomas, 118 benign nevi). Input did not include questionnaire answers.
Ludwig Maximilian University (LMU) Datab.notes: Sensitivity increased from 73% (initial algorithm) to 88%. Specificity dropped from 83% (initial algorithm) to approximately 79%.

architecture_used.main_model: Rule-based algorithm incorporating fractal and classical image analysis

features_extracted: Circularity of the lesion extracted from the fractal map
architecture_details.additional_inputs: Patient questionnaire answers regarding lesion characteristics (e.g., duration, visibility, accompanying symptoms like fever/pain/itch/bleeding, changes in size/color)

training_details.calibration_method: Calibration using training data to add new rules and improve existing ones
training_details.training_dataset: 233 pigmented and nonpigmented melanocytic and nonmelanocytic lesions from Catharina Hospital Eindhoven
training_details.recalibration_purpose: To accommodate nonpigmented skin conditions, increase specificity for nonpigmented lesions, and increase sensitivity for melanoma

classification_or_output_head.logic: High risk if the algorithm identifies the lesion as malignant or premalignant; medium or low risk otherwise

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SkinVision mHealth application algorithm (rule-based algorithm incorporating fractal and classical image analysis)
Original algorithm (dedicated to melanocytic lesion analysis and melanoma detection, before recalibration)
preprocessing_techniques: Image acquisition in video mode to ensure focused images without shadows and complete lesion containment
Quality control during acquisition to ensure images are focused, without shadows, and completely contain the lesion of interest
No images were excluded from the study due to low quality to measure algorithm performance in a real-use setup

Exclusion criteria for future studies include lesions with ulcerations or bleeding, those surrounded by mottled or extremely tanned skin, lesions localized in skin folds and hairy areas, and lesions with other skin lesions in close proximity

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper did not report using any data augmentation techniques (such as geometric transforms, color transforms, GANs, or synthetic data generation) during training or dataset construction. The algorithm was a rule-based system calibrated using a fixed dataset of 233 lesions, and the evaluation was performed on a separate test set of 108 lesions without augmentation.

conclusions: The recalibrated SkinVision mHealth application algorithm achieved a sensitivity of 80% and a specificity of 78% in detecting (pre)malignant conditions on a test set of 108 lesions from the Eindhoven Database.
The app adds value to diagnosis tools by addressing both pigmented and nonpigmented lesions together and detecting signs of malignancy with high sensitivity.

Although less accurate than a dermatologist's clinical eye, the app may offer valuable support to nondermatologists (such as general practitioners and surgeons) who are less familiar with differentiating between benign and malignant lesions.
Incorporating patient questionnaire answers into the algorithm significantly improved performance, increasing specificity by 22% and sensitivity by 9% compared to using image analysis alone.

The recalibrated algorithm demonstrated a significant increase in sensitivity (from 73% to 88%) and a slight drop in specificity (from 83% to ~79%) when evaluated on the Ludwig Maximilian University (LMU) database for melanocytic lesions.
The algorithm correctly identified all melanomas as high risk in the Eindhoven test set.

A limitation of the study was the low number of squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) cases (n=3) and limited sample sizes for certain benign subgroups (lichen planus-like keratosis, sebaceous hyperplasia, and verruca vulgaris), which may interfere with the analysis.

The app may cause unnecessary panic in patients due to the misclassification of harmless lesions like verruca seborrhoica and lentigo solaris as higher risk, suggesting that future questionnaire adjustments could help differentiate these benign conditions.
important_remarks: The study has a small sample size for malignant lesions, particularly squamous cell carcinoma (n=3) and melanoma (n=2 in the Eindhoven test set), which limits the statistical power for these specific subtypes.

Several benign lesion subgroups had very low sample sizes in the test set (e.g., sebaceous hyperplasia n=3, verruca vulgaris n=1, lichen planus-like keratosis n=3), potentially interfering with the reliability of performance metrics for these classes.

The algorithm misclassified harmless lesions such as verruca seborrhoica and lentigo solaris as higher risk (high or medium) in 55% and 71% of cases respectively, which may cause unnecessary patient panic.

Image acquisition quality was problematic for lesions with ulcerations/bleeding, those surrounded by mottled or extremely tanned skin, lesions in skin folds/hairy areas, or those with other lesions in close proximity; these are now defined as exclusion criteria for future studies.
The study did not exclude low-quality images to simulate real-world use, but acknowledged that this setup revealed difficulties in capturing focused images for certain lesion types.

The algorithm's performance on the Eindhoven test set was evaluated against clinical diagnosis for most benign lesions, with only a subset (n=6) having histopathological confirmation, introducing potential verification bias for benign cases.

The original algorithm (dedicated to melanocytic lesions) performed poorly on the mixed dataset (sensitivity 25%, specificity 75%), highlighting the necessity of recalibration for nonpigmented lesions.

Incorporating patient questionnaire answers significantly improved algorithm performance (sensitivity increased from 71% to 80%, specificity from 56% to 78%), demonstrating the value of multimodal input.
The study was conducted at a single center (Catharina Hospital Eindhoven), limiting the generalizability of the results to other populations or clinical settings without external validation.
